front: Artist: articulated the "Grand Style" / "Grand Manner" in a lecture series called Discourses on Art, emphasizing idealizing subjects and following Renaissance art
back: Joshua Reynolds

front: Painting: a three-year-old child dressed to look like a Holbein portrait of Henry VIII, with two dogs nearby
back: Master Crewe as Henry VIII (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Painting: myopic literary critic in brown holds a book directly up to his nose
back: Portrait of Giuseppe Baretti (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Artist: Nathaniel Hone satirized this artist's close friendship with Angelica Kauffman in a painting called The Conjurer
back: Joshua Reynolds

front: Artist: a myth claims this artist's Eighth Discourse on color theory caused Gainsborough to paint The Blue Boy
back: Joshua Reynolds

front: Painting: the award statuette in the film All About Eve (the 'Sarah Siddons Award') is modeled after this painting
back: Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Painting: three grandnieces of Horace Walpole in identical white gowns and gray wigs embroidering silk — an allusion to the Fates
back: The Ladies Waldegrave (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Painting: Polynesian man in the adlocutio pose; called 'Britain's first grand portrayal of a non-white subject'; jointly purchased by National Portrait Gallery and Getty Museum (2023)
back: Portrait of Omai / Portrait of Mai (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Painting: in Edith Wharton's The House of Mirth, Lily Bart reenacts this Reynolds portrait in a tableaux vivants
back: Mrs. Lloyd (Joshua Reynolds)

front: Artist: Pre-Raphaelites mocked him with the nickname 'Sir Sloshua'
back: Joshua Reynolds

front: Artist: described Velazquez's Portrait of Innocent X as 'the finest picture in Rome'
back: Joshua Reynolds

front: Artist: helped Angelica Kauffman escape a fraudulent marriage; they exchanged portraits; Nathaniel Hone mocked their friendship in The Conjurer
back: Joshua Reynolds
